title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 5 (Fragment 3)
text:
Almost at once, Aunt Gina shot out of the kitchen again. She had canceled the ordinary supper and started on a new one in honor of Chrestomanci. Six people were sent out at once for cakes and fruit, and two more for lettuce and cheese. Paolo, Corinna and Lucia were caught as they came in chatting from school and told to go at once to the butcher’s. But, at this point, Rinaldo erupted furiously from the Scriptorium.

“What do you mean, sending all the kids off like this!” he bawled from the gallery. “We’re up to our ears in war-spells here. I need copiers!”

Aunt Gina put her hands on her hips and bawled back at him. “And I need steak! Don’t you stand up there cheeking me, Rinaldo Montana! English people always eat steak, so steak I must have!”

“Then cut pieces off the cats!” screamed Rinaldo. “I need Corinna and Lucia up here!”

“I tell you they are going to run after me for once!” yelled Aunt Gina.

“Dear me,” said Chrestomanci, wandering into the yard. “What a very Italian scene! Can I help in any way?” He nodded and smiled from Aunt Gina to Rinaldo. Both of them smiled back, Rinaldo at his most charming.

“You would agree I need copiers, sir, wouldn’t you?” he said.

“Bah!” said Aunt Gina. “Rinaldo turns on the charm and I get left to struggle alone! As usual! All right. Because it’s war-spells, Paolo and Tonino can go for the steak. But wait while I write you a note, or you’ll come back with something no one can chew.”

“So glad to be of service,” Chrestomanci murmured, and turned away to greet Elizabeth, who came racing down from the gallery waving a sheaf of music and fell into his arms. The heads of the five little cousins Elizabeth had been teaching stared wonderingly over the gallery rail. “Elizabeth!” said Chrestomanci. “Looking younger than ever!” Tonino stared as wonderingly as his cousins. His mother was laughing and crying at once. He could not follow the torrent of English speech. “Virtue,” he heard, and “war” and, before long, the inevitable “ Angel of Caprona .” He was still staring when Aunt Gina stuck her note into his hand and told him to make haste.

As they hurried to the butcher’s, Tonino said to Paolo, “I didn’t know Mother knew anyone like Chrestomanci.”

“Neither did I,” Paolo confessed. He was only a year older than Tonino, after all, and it seemed that Chrestomanci had last been in Caprona a very long time ago. “Perhaps he’s come to find the words to the Angel ,” Paolo suggested. “I hope so. I don’t want Rinaldo to have to go away and fight.”

“Or Marco,” Tonino agreed. “Or Carlo or Luigi or even Domenico.”

Because of Aunt Gina’s note, the butcher treated them with great respect, “Tell her this is the last good steak she’ll see, if war is declared,” he said, and he passed them each a heavy, squashy pink armload.

They arrived back with their armfuls just as a cab set down Uncle Umberto, puffing and panting, outside the Casa gate. “I am right, Chrestomanci is here? Eh, Paolo?” Uncle Umberto asked Tonino.

Both boys nodded. It seemed easier than explaining that Paolo was Tonino.

“Good, good!” exclaimed Uncle Umberto and surged into the Casa, where he found Chrestomanci just crossing the yard. “The Angel of Caprona ,” Uncle Umberto said to him eagerly. “Could you—?”

“My dear Umberto,” said Chrestomanci, shaking his hand warmly, “everyone here is asking me that. For that matter, so was everyone in the Casa Petrocchi too. And I’m afraid I know no more than you do. But I shall think about it, don’t worry.”

“If you could find just a line, to get us started,” Uncle Umberto said pleadingly.

“I will do my best—” Chrestomanci was saying, when, with a great clattering of heels, Rosa shot past. From the look on her face, she had seen Marco arriving. “I promise you that,” Chrestomanci said, as his head turned to see what Rosa was running for.

vocabulary.definition: to shout something loudly in a noisy way.
vocabulary.example: ” he bawled from the gallery.

vocabulary.definition: a person whose job is to sell meat in a shop.
vocabulary.example:
”

Because of Aunt Gina’s note, the butcher treated them with great respect, “Tell her this is the last good steak she’ll see, if war is declared,” he said, and he passed them each a heavy, squashy pink armload.

vocabulary.definition: to decide that a planned event will not take place.
vocabulary.example: She had canceled the ordinary supper and started on a new one in honor of Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: the power or quality of pleasing or attracting others.
vocabulary.example: Both of them smiled back, Rinaldo at his most charming.

vocabulary.definition: to speak disrespectfully or impudently to someone.
vocabulary.example: Don’t you stand up there cheeking me, Rinaldo Montana!

vocabulary.definition: to admit or declare something, often reluctantly.
vocabulary.example:
”

“Neither did I,” Paolo confessed.

vocabulary.definition: a person or device that copies something.
vocabulary.example: I need copiers!

vocabulary.definition: a sudden, often violent, outburst.
vocabulary.example: But, at this point, Rinaldo erupted furiously from the Scriptorium.

vocabulary.definition: in an extremely angry way.
vocabulary.example: But, at this point, Rinaldo erupted furiously from the Scriptorium.

vocabulary.definition: a balcony or upper floor inside a building; a place for viewing.
vocabulary.example: ” he bawled from the gallery.

vocabulary.definition: to say hello or welcome someone.
vocabulary.example:
”

“So glad to be of service,” Chrestomanci murmured, and turned away to greet Elizabeth, who came racing down from the gallery waving a sheaf of music and fell into his arms.

vocabulary.definition: high respect or esteem; an act of showing respect.
vocabulary.example: She had canceled the ordinary supper and started on a new one in honor of Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: certain to happen; unavoidable.
vocabulary.example: “Virtue,” he heard, and “war” and, before long, the inevitable “ Angel of Caprona .

vocabulary.definition: to speak quietly and softly.
vocabulary.example:
”

“So glad to be of service,” Chrestomanci murmured, and turned away to greet Elizabeth, who came racing down from the gallery waving a sheaf of music and fell into his arms.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that shows needing or asking for something urgently.

”

“If you could find just a line, to get us started,” Uncle Umberto said pleadingly.

vocabulary.example:
”

Because of Aunt Gina’s note, the butcher treated them with great respect, “Tell her this is the last good steak she’ll see, if war is declared,” he said, and he passed them each a heavy, squashy pink armload.

vocabulary.example:
”

Because of Aunt Gina’s note, the butcher treated them with great respect, “Tell her this is the last good steak she’ll see, if war is declared,” he said, and he passed them each a heavy, squashy pink armload.

vocabulary.definition: to try very hard to do something difficult.
vocabulary.example: “Rinaldo turns on the charm and I get left to struggle alone!

vocabulary.definition: an evening meal.
vocabulary.example: She had canceled the ordinary supper and started on a new one in honor of Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: to move suddenly and powerfully forward or upward.
vocabulary.example: ” exclaimed Uncle Umberto and surged into the Casa, where he found Chrestomanci just crossing the yard.

vocabulary.definition: a strong and fast-moving stream of water or words.
vocabulary.example: He could not follow the torrent of English speech.

vocabulary.definition: magical spells related to war or battle.
vocabulary.example: “We’re up to our ears in war-spells here.
